recent_posts.caption: We keep growing together! Important win!
Keep it going! 🟡🔵

Longevity and high performance don’t happen by chance. They’re built through daily, practical recovery.

It’s not a secret. It’s a routine.
Back where we belong. Now back to work! 💪🏽
We rise through hard work. 💪

A new chapter for me and @herbalife. 
The next era of wellness is Pro2col.

Find out more - link in bio.

@herbalifeceo
Another step forward. Let’s keep going. 💪
Locked in.

Proud to be part of this project with @alnassr and @vhils.

Available now. Link in my Stories.

recent_posts.caption: If nature seems boring, you’re not wild enough. Welcome to All Conditions Gear.
A new icon takes center stage. The A’Two available May two.

The odds were stacked against us.

In this tournament, they’re stacked against everyone else.

The run of a lifetime.

@keely.hodgkinson breaks the 800m indoor world record, set on the day she was born nearly 24 years ago.

Fast? Try flawless.

Choi Minjeong (@chmj10021) and @gilli_kim make a statement in the 3000m Relay, upsetting the field for straight Gold on the Short Track.

#MilanoCortina2026 #Olympics

profile.biography: 🚀 🌎  Exploring the universe and our home planet. Verification: nasa.gov/socialmedia

recent_posts.caption:
What a view!

You're looking through the cupola, the International Space Station's "window to the world." The cupola has seven large windows that give astronauts on the @ISS a view of Earth unlike any other. The cupola's not just for show, though—it also holds the workstation for the Canadarm2 robotic arm, which maneuvers and grapples equipment, spacecraft, and even astronauts outside the space station.

The cupola was added to the station on Feb. 15, 2010, as part of Expedition 22. This image was taken by astronaut Karen Nyberg in 2013.

Image description: Earth is seen through the cupola of the International Space Station. Three trapezoidal windows, with part of a circular window at the bottom of the image, look upon Earth's blue seas and white clouds. Various hardware is seen lining the inside of the station.

Credit: NASA/Karen Nyberg

#NASA #Space #SpaceStation #Earth

Behold the lunar eclipse

On Tuesday, March 3, Earth passed directly between the Sun and our Moon, creating a total lunar eclipse that could be seen in North America and eastern Asia. During a total lunar eclipse, some sunlight still reaches the Moon's surface after interacting with Earth's atmosphere. Longer wavelengths of light, like reds and oranges, pass through the atmosphere, while shorter-wavelength blues and violets are scattered. 

This causes the Moon to turn a dark red—in other words, a Blood Moon. This is the same effect that turns the sky pink, orange, and red at sunrise and sunset!

These photos of the total lunar eclipse were taken from New Orleans, the home of NASA's Michoud Assembly Facility. Also known as "America's rocket factory," Michoud is where we manufacture and assemble the core stages for the Space Launch System rockets which are powering NASA's Artemis missions.

Image description: This composite image shows four views of a total lunar eclipse in a 2x2 grid. From top-left to bottom-right, the Moon is seen increasingly in shadow as the eclipse progresses. In the final, bottom-right image, the Moon is faintly visible with a reddish-orange glow.

Credit: NASA/Eric Bordelon

#NASA #Moon #LunarEclipse

Like day and night

Our @NASAHubble telescope snapped this photo of an interacting pair of galaxies, known as Arp 140, sitting side by side. This pair is located in the constellation Cetus, and while each one looks vastly different from the other, they are still both galaxies—just different types with different structures.

The sparkly blue galaxy on the left, NGC 275, is a barred spiral galaxy, which is simply a spiral galaxy with a bar of stars running through the center. The bar in this one looks like a glowing vertical stripe of white light. Spiral galaxies are known for their spiral arms, and the arms in barred spiral galaxies typically start at either end of the bar of stars.

The hazy galaxy on the right, NGC 274, is a lenticular galaxy, which is a cross between spiral and elliptical galaxy structures. Like spiral galaxies, lenticular galaxies have a central bulge of stars and a flattened disc-like shape. But like elliptical galaxies, lenticular galaxies have little gas and dust and are made primarily of old stars.

Image description: The two galaxies of Arp 140 dominate the image, sitting side by side and overlapping slightly. The galaxy on the left is a textured, dynamic spiral galaxy glowing blue from the stars within it. The galaxy on the right is a lenticular galaxy, appearing as a diffuse, warm glow. Glowing points of distant stars and galaxies dot the black background of space.

Credit: NASA/ESA/R. Foley (University of California - Santa Cruz)/Processing: Gladys Kober (NASA/Catholic University of America)

#NASA #Galaxy #Hubble #BFF
Tonight’s Moon is in its red era #TotalLunarEclipse

NASA is increasing the cadence of future Artemis missions.

The Artemis III mission, now in 2027, will be designed to test out systems and operational capabilities in low Earth orbit as preparation for an Artemis IV landing in 2028. This new mission will endeavor to include a rendezvous and docking with one or both commercial landers from SpaceX and Blue Origin.

This change will accelerate the flight rate of the Artemis missions and enable a safer and more reliable launch cadence.

There will be no changes to the Artemis II mission. In this video, we see a timelapse of the Artemis II rocket and spacecraft rolling back to the Vehicle Assembly Building at @NASAKennedy atop of the crawler-transporter on Wednesday, Feb. 25. Teams will conduct work and repairs on the Artemis II vehicle before returning it to Launch Pad 39B to launch on its crewed mission around the Moon no earlier than April 2026.

Credit: NASA
Thumbnail Credit: NASA/John Kraus

How big would the piece of gum have to be? 🤔

Our @NASAChandraXray observatory spotted, for the first time, a young Sun-like star blowing a bubble! Winds from the star’s surface are blowing up the bubble – called an “astrosphere” – and filling it with hot gas as it expands into much cooler galactic gas and dust surrounding the star. The astrosphere completely surrounds the star.

Earth's Sun has a similar bubble around it, which scientists call the heliosphere, created by the solar wind. It extends far beyond the planets in our solar system and protects Earth from cosmic radiation. By studying this star’s astrosphere, we can learn about the Sun’s – namely, its shape and how it has changed over billions of years as the Sun evolves and moves through the galaxy.

Image description: A star seen in infrared and X-ray light. There is a hazy purple bubble that reaches upward and below that, dust wings that look like a moth’s wings.

Credit: X-ray: NASA/CXC/Johns Hopkins Univ./C.M. Lisse et al.; Infrared: NASA/ESA/STIS; Image Processing: NASA/CXC/SAO/N. Wolk

#NASA #Telescope #Stars #Sun

Teams have successfully transported the Artemis II rocket and spacecraft from Launch Pad 39B to the Vehicle Assembly Building at @NASAKennedy.

While in the assembly building, technicians will troubleshoot the helium flow issue noticed after testing last weekend.

The April launch window for the crewed lunar mission is potentially preserved, pending the outcome of data findings and repair efforts.

Image description: NASA’s crawler-transporter 2, carrying NASA’s Artemis II SLS (Space Launch System) rocket with the Orion spacecraft, arrives on Feb. 25, 2026, inside the Vehicle Assembly Building (VAB) at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida. The perspective of the image is from above the rocket, looking downward. The orange core stage stands between two white solid rocket boosters and is topped with the white Orion spacecraft. The rocket stands between illuminated platforms inside of the VAB. Parts of the VAB are in shadow. 

Credit: NASA/Cory Huston

Feeling over the weather?

This pair of images taken by astronauts aboard the @ISS show what storms can look like from above the clouds.

Our astronauts in low Earth orbit have a unique vantage point that helps scientists on the ground study Earth. Storm observations can lead to improved weather models, and photographs of the planet can record how it changes over time.

Image descriptions:
1. An image of the Milky Way galaxy, rich with stars, above Earth as seen from the International Space Station. A patch of clouds within Earth's atmosphere glows bright white from a lightning strike below them. The lightning is so bright that it illuminates the space station’s solar panels at the top of the image. Credit: NASA/Kjell Lindgren
2. An image of Tropical Storm Erick's cloud tops glowing from lightning below them. They appear as a dramatic steely swirl amidst pure darkness. Credit: NASA/Nichole Ayers

#NASA #ISS #Astrophotography #Storm #Lightning

Spotting Jupiter's second-largest moon

Meet Callisto. Nearly 3,000 miles wide, it's about the size of Mercury and larger than every moon in our solar system except for Ganymede and Titan. It's spotted with a number of impact craters, collected over billions of years—including the massive Valhalla crater seen here on Callisto's left side.

This photo is a combination of images taken by our Voyager 1 spacecraft as it flew by Callisto in 1979. Launched in 1977, Voyager 1 has been soaring for nearly 50 years. It crossed into interstellar space in 2012 and is the most distant object made by humanity.

Image description: Roughly half of the moon Callisto, a greenish-yellow sphere pocked with countless craters, is seen from orbit. A particularly large crater forms a bullseye shape on its left side, with concentric rings surrounding it.

Credit: NASA/JPL-Caltech

#NASA #Space #Universe #Callisto

Strike a pose 🦚

This image snapped by @NASAChandraxray contains an exceptionally massive spiral galaxy. Known as NGC 6872, this galaxy is found in the constellation Pavo, or the Peacock, and is located about 300 million light-years from Earth. This behemoth measures about 522,000 light-years across, which is over five times the width of our Milky Way galaxy.

A much smaller galaxy is visible near the center of this image. This galaxy passed through NGC 6872 more than 100 million years ago—helping give it the unusual shape it has today.

Image description: The galaxy NGC 6872 dominates the scene. It has two long arms stretched out from its center, where they connect and wrap around a bright central point. A nearby galaxy, the smaller patch of bright light to the left of the main galaxy, is distorting its shape. Other bright stars are seen within and around the arms. The galaxy itself is a vivid purple and fades to dusty blue along the outer arms. Distant stars and galaxies are shown as points of light in the black background of space.

Credit: X-ray: NASA/CXC/SAO; Optical: NASA/ESA/STScI; Image Processing: NASA/CXC/SAO/J. Schmidt, L. Frattare, and J. Major

#NASA #Chandra #Universe #Galaxy #Peacock

We are targeting no earlier than March 6 for the launch of Artemis II, pending completion of required work at the launch pad and analysis of test data.

The Artemis astronauts have entered quarantine to remain in good health before the mission. They will fly to @NASAKennedy about five days before launch.

Image description: The silhouette of the Artemis II rocket and spacecraft and the silhouette of the trussed structure of the mobile launcher stand together at Launch Complex 39B at NASA’s Kennedy Space Center. The sun is setting in the background, creating a gradient of gold to orange to blue. The dark silhouette of the Artemis II rocket and spacecraft stands between the silhouette of three lightning towers and one water tower.

Credit: NASA/Sam Lott

Amazing views from the space station are a shore thing 🐟⁣

NASA astronaut @zenanaut took this picture of the Arabian Sea and India’s west coast while the @ISS orbited 259 miles above. We can see the usual city lights, but there’s also something interesting in the water: A line of green light along the shore. What is that?⁣

Those are fishing boats, and they’re using green lights designed to attract squid, shrimp, sardines, and mackerel!⁣

When we study nighttime light – whether we use photographs of Earth from the space station like this one or data from satellites – we have an opportunity to learn about human behavior, natural disasters, and how the planet changes over time. For example, scientists can research how artificial lights influences nature, how urban areas expand, or even how glaciers move.⁣

Image description: A view of India’s west coast from the International Space Station. Green lights on fishing boats line the water by the shore. On the land at right, lights sparkle in yellow and white. There are pockets of dense light where cities are – for example, in the bottom right corner and just above center.⁣

Credit: NASA/Zena Cardman⁣

#NASA #CityLights #NightPhotography #India
